name: Lane Trap Rock Quarry
short_description: Historic trap rock quarry near Granby offering exposure to igneous minerals and feldspar crystals in a worked Massachusetts quarry setting.
description:
Lane Trap Rock Quarry is a historical quarry site in Hampshire County, Massachusetts, near the town of Granby. Trap rock (diabase or basalt) quarries in this region are associated with the Mesozoic-age Deerfield Basin and represent intrusive igneous activity from rifting during the Triassic period. The quarry exposes dark igneous rock with visible feldspar phenocrysts, quartz, and mica minerals typical of trap rock formations.

The site sits at 478 feet elevation with moderate sloping terrain characteristic of western Massachusetts piedmont geology. The quarry face and surrounding rock outcrops provide direct access to mineral specimens without extensive hiking. This is an excellent location for collectors interested in igneous mineralogy and understanding New England's basement rocks.

Access and collection practices at this site depend on current ownership and operational status. Many Massachusetts quarries are private property or actively worked commercial sites. Always verify current access permissions before visiting, as quarry operations and land use change over time. Contact the town of Granby or Hampshire County assessor's office to confirm public access status and any collection allowances.

The moderate slope and rock terrain require sturdy footwear and careful footing around quarry walls and loose stone. Spring through fall offers the most accessible conditions, though the site is potentially visitable year-round depending on weather and ground conditions.
history: This trap rock quarry is part of the Deerfield Basin diabase intrusions that formed during Triassic rifting approximately 200 million years ago. Igneous magma injected into sedimentary host rock, cooling slowly to produce the coarse-grained feldspar and quartz crystals visible in trap rock quarries throughout western Massachusetts. Historic quarrying operations in this region supplied dimension stone and crushed rock for road construction and building materials throughout the 19th and 20th centuries.
tips: Examine the quarry face and nearby talus blocks for well-formed feldspar crystals, often showing bright white or pink coloration. Bring a 10x hand lens to identify mica sheets and quartz grains within the dark trap rock matrix. Look for loose blocks at the quarry base where weathering has freed mineral specimens. Always obtain permission from the current quarry operator or landowner before collecting, and never enter active quarry work areas or approach vertical quarry walls.
terrain_notes: The site is at 478 feet elevation with moderate slope terrain typical of western Massachusetts bedrock uplands. Dark trap rock outcrop and quarry stone dominate the landscape with minimal vegetation on exposed rock faces. Walking distance from parking is typically short, though the exact layout depends on current quarry operations and public access points. Footing is uneven and rocky; sturdy hiking boots with good ankle support are essential due to loose stone fragments and potential sharp edges.
best_season: May - October. Late spring through early fall provides dry ground, stable footing, and clear visibility of mineral specimens on quarry faces. Summer heat is moderate in western Massachusetts. Fall offers comfortable temperatures and low humidity. Winter precipitation and frost can create hazardous footing on wet rock and ice.
written_directions: From Granby center, take Route 189 east toward East Granby. Continue for approximately 2 miles. Lane Trap Rock Quarry is located north of Route 189; watch for quarry signage or access roads on the north side of the highway. GPS coordinates 42.301243, -72.527078 mark the site. Approach via local roads off Route 189 or Route 20. Verify exact access roads with town of Granby, as quarry property boundaries and public access points may be limited.
road_conditions: Local roads accessing the quarry are paved or well-maintained dirt; passenger vehicles should be sufficient. Road conditions may vary with season and quarry operations.
parking_notes: Parking depends on current quarry operations and public access status. There may be limited informal parking near the quarry entrance or designated public access points. Always check with the quarry operator or town before assuming parking availability.
collection_rules: Access and collection permissions must be obtained from the current quarry operator or property owner prior to visiting. Many trap rock quarries in Massachusetts are private commercial operations or inactive sites with restricted public access. No collection without explicit written permission. Respect all posted signs and barriers. Do not enter active quarry work areas or approach quarry equipment.
rules:
Obtain permission from property owner or quarry operator before accessing
No collection without explicit written permission from operator
Do not enter active quarry operations or work areas
Respect all posted signs and barriers
Stay clear of quarry walls and dangerous drop-offs
Do not remove material from quarry face walls
nearest_services: Granby town center, approximately 2-3 miles south. Gas, food, and water available in downtown Granby.
accessibility_notes: Uneven rocky terrain with loose stone fragments and sharp edges throughout. No wheelchair access. Steep quarry walls and drop-offs present significant hazards. Sturdy hiking boots with ankle support required. Not suitable for visitors with limited mobility or those unable to navigate rough, uneven ground. Children should be closely supervised due to quarry hazards.

faq.question: Can you rockhound at Lane Trap Rock Quarry in Granby, Massachusetts?
faq.answer: Lane Trap Rock Quarry is private property with restricted public access. Permission from the current quarry operator or property owner is required before visiting or collecting. Contact the town of Granby or Hampshire County assessor's office to identify the current owner and request access. Many Massachusetts quarries are active commercial operations with no public collection allowed.

faq.question: What minerals can you find at Lane Trap Rock Quarry?
faq.answer: This trap rock quarry exposes feldspar (white to pink crystals), quartz, and mica minerals typical of Triassic-age diabase intrusions. Feldspar phenocrysts are the most abundant and visually striking specimens. The dark trap rock matrix contains small quartz grains and mica sheets visible with a hand lens. These igneous minerals reflect the quarry's geological origin in the Deerfield Basin.

faq.question: What is the best season to visit Lane Trap Rock Quarry?
faq.answer: May through October offers the best conditions, with dry ground and stable footing essential for navigating the rocky, uneven terrain. Summer and fall temperatures are moderate in western Massachusetts. Winter precipitation, frost, and ice create hazardous footing on exposed rock. Spring may have muddy conditions after snowmelt. Always verify access and operational status before planning a visit.

faq.question: Do you need a permit to collect at Lane Trap Rock Quarry in Massachusetts?
faq.answer: Yes, you must obtain explicit written permission from the property owner or quarry operator before collecting. This is private property with active or historically active quarry operations. There is no public collection permit or fee system for this site. Contact the town of Granby to identify the current owner and request access authorization.

faq.question: What tools should you bring rockhounding at Lane Trap Rock Quarry?
faq.answer: Bring sturdy hiking boots with ankle support, a 10x hand lens for identifying mica and quartz grains, and basic collecting tools like a rock hammer and chisel if permitted. A headlamp or flashlight is helpful for examining quarry faces and crevices. Gloves protect hands from sharp rock edges. Always check with the operator about which collecting tools are allowed on-site.

faq.question: Is Lane Trap Rock Quarry rockhounding site beginner-friendly?
faq.answer: This site is not beginner-friendly due to private access restrictions, hazardous quarry terrain, steep walls, and loose stone. Visitors require prior permission and experience navigating uneven rocky ground around quarry edges. The age recommendation is 12+ with adult supervision. Beginners should start at public rockhounding locations with established access before attempting private quarry sites.
